  - description: Moodle là một Hệ thống Quản lý Học tập Online (LMS) mở nguồn được sử dụng rộng rãi trong các khóa học và chương trình giáo dục. Đây là một nền tảng trực tuyến mà giảng viên và sinh viên có thể giao tiếp, quản lý khóa học từ thiết lập ban đầu đến sau khi kết thúc. Moodle bao gồm nhiều tính năng quan trọng như phân phối tài liệu, quản lý bài tập và hoạt động học tập.  Hệ thống này hỗ trợ mọi loại khóa học khác nhau, từ Quản lý Dự án Capstone cho đến Khóa học Máy tính Tính toán và Robot Viễn cảnh. Moodle thường được tích hợp với các công cụ khác để tạo ra một chiến lược giáo dục toàn diện. Đặc biệt, Moodle là nền tảng trực tuyến mở nguồn, giúp giáo viên dễ dàng quản lý khóa học và giao tiếp với sinh viên. Mục đích chính của Course ID là đảm bảo sự rõ ràng và chính xác khi đề cập đến khóa học trong các tài liệu học thuật, hệ thống quản trị hành chính và danh mục khóa học, đóng vai trò là một công cụ cơ bản trong việc tổ chức và tham chiếu tất cả các tài liệu khóa học.   - description: The entity **Workload** for the course BAA00003 (also referred to as Digital Image and Video Processing) is defined as the total time commitment required from students to successfully complete the course. It is consistently described as the aggregate sum of contact hours and independent study hours. Contact hours are divided into time spent in lectures and in laboratory sessions, while independent study encompasses self-directed activities such as self-study, examination preparation, and other academic tasks.  There are significant numerical inconsistencies in the reported figures, indicating potential variations in reporting standards, different course iterations, or distinct interpretations. The most frequently and consistently reported breakdown specifies a total workload of approximately 178.75 hours. This total is composed of roughly 37.5 hours dedicated to lectures, 25 hours for laboratory sessions, and 116.25 hours allocated to self-study and exam preparation.  Alternative descriptions present conflicting totals and breakdowns. One suggests a total of approximately 140 hours, with about 25 lecture hours, 25 lab hours, and 87.5 hours for self-study. Another highly divergent description cites a total of 550 hours, comprising 0 lecture hours, 250 laboratory hours, and 300 hours for self-study. A further variation notes 0 lecture hours, 150 laboratory hours, and 180 self-study hours. Despite these numerical discrepancies, all sources uniformly define the core conceptual structure of the workload entity.<SEP>The total hours for the course, including approximately 25 lecture hours and 57.5 hours for self-study and preparation.<SEP>Workload refers to the total hours required for the course, including approximately 25 lecture hours and 57.5 hours for self-study and exam preparation.<SEP>The workload includes lecture hours and self-study hours for the course.

ID: Pháp luật đại cương
  - entity_id: Pháp luật đại cương
  - entity_type: course
  - description: Pháp luật đại cương (Basic Law) là một khóa học giới thiệu về các chủ đề pháp luật cơ bản và bao gồm nhiều lĩnh vực khác nhau. Đây là môn học bắt buộc trong chương trình Giáo dục chung, đặc biệt cho sinh viên ngành Khoa học Máy tính và Công nghệ Thông tin.  Pháp luật đại cương cũng được liệt kê như một tài liệu đọc để học hỏi, nhằm cung cấp kiến thức cơ bản về pháp luật cần thiết cho các chuyên gia công nghệ thông tin. Nó là một phần quan trọng của chương trình đào tạo cao đẳng ngành Công nghệ Thông tin và đại học.  Môn học này không chỉ giới thiệu các kiến thức về luật pháp mà còn được lựa chọn làm một phần của khóa tuyển sinh năm 2015, thể hiện tầm quan trọng của nó trong lĩnh vực học thuật và công việc.<SEP>Pháp luật đại cương là một môn học liên quan đến các cơ bản của pháp luật, bao gồm nội dung về quyền và nghĩa vụ, tổ chức hành pháp, hình sự, dân sự, thương mại.

ID: Course Topics
  - entity_id: Course Topics
  - entity_type: content
  - description: Course Topics refer to the specific subjects, modules, and activities covered within an academic or training program. They form the core material that students discuss in study groups and engage with during class participation. The provided descriptions reveal a wide array of distinct courses across different disciplines, each with its own unique set of topics.  In computer science and engineering, several specialized courses are detailed. One course covers automata theory, formal languages, and computational models. Another focuses on signals and systems, including an overview of signals, digital signal sampling, discrete systems, Z transform, Fourier and other transforms, noise filtering, data embedding, signal compression, and audio signal processing. In cybersecurity, topics range from security introductions to advanced cryptographic areas like Blockchain and Quantum Cryptography. For software development, subjects include introduction to modeling, business process modeling, user requirement modeling, analysis modeling, design modeling, software testing specifications, model driven approaches, and model validation. A specific Software Architecture course covers technical subjects such as patterns, middleware, and architectural styles.  In data-focused fields, an Introduction to Data Science course has a structured list of subjects, while a dedicated Data Visualization course includes modules on introduction, principles, analysis, and design. For digital image processing (DIP), topics encompass introduction to DIP, fundamental concepts, color transformations, geometric transformations, smoothing, edge detection, segmentation, video segmentation, Fourier Transform, Karhunen-Loève Transformation, image compression, and seminar projects.  In business and general education, the topics vary significantly. A course for IT startups covers practical business areas like business plan creation, team building, product development, marketing, fundraising, and strategy adaptation. Other courses emphasize personal, social, and cultural themes. One is structured around eight modules: Leisure and lifestyle; Important firsts; At rest, at work; Special occasions; Appearances; Time off; Ambitions and dreams; and Countries and cultures. Another related course covers Socialising, Things you can’t live without, Future society, An amazing story, Rules and freedom, and Dilemmas.  Finally, the term also applies to the procedural components of an Industrial-based Thesis, encompassing activities like registration, proposal submission, and defense.

  - description: Course Goals are the intended learning outcomes or objectives that students are expected to achieve upon successful completion of a specific course. They outline the skills, knowledge, competencies, and abilities students will develop or gain by the end of the instructional period.  These goals apply to a wide variety of academic disciplines and specific courses. The objective covers fundamental programming proficiency, requiring students to describe and use basic programming concepts in languages such as C/C++. It extends into more advanced areas of computer science, including the application of graph theory algorithms to solve practical problems. Furthermore, G1 involves systems analysis and software engineering, asking students to analyze complex problems, generate solution requirements, and propose appropriate software development models for projects.  A significant component of G1 is the development of professional skills. It requires students to apply personal and teamwork skills to collaborative development tasks, such as creating a real web application. ID: G4
  - entity_id: G4
  - entity_type: major
  - description: G4 is a Course Learning Objective (CLO) that appears within a curriculum to define specific, assessable student competencies across a variety of technical and computer science domains. The objective encompasses a broad range of skills, from foundational programming to complex software engineering and theoretical application. It requires students to write well-organized C/C++ programs to solve basic problems and to develop, analyze, and deploy a small to medium-sized web application. In a systems context, G4 outlines learning objectives related to understanding an operating system's role, its development process, and its different types.  For more advanced and collaborative work, G4 involves practicing software engineering by developing a 75,000-line code software project as part of a 7-person team. It also includes the defense of a project through a poster presentation. Thematically, G4 covers several analytical and evaluative competencies. It increases sensitivity in acquiring and evaluating the value of information, provides an understanding of the practical uses of graph theory in various fields, and requires students to express the meaning and application of data mining problems in real-world contexts.<SEP>G4 is a learning objective to understand wireless network security.<SEP>G4 is a learning objective for students to identify and classify different measures and mechanisms of differential privacy.<SEP>G4 is a learning objective (CLO) for practicing and simulating a robotics vision system.<SEP>G4 is a learning objective to practice the principle of separation of concern into image and video processing.<SEP>A learning objective to practice computer programming using the WebGL toolkit.<SEP>G4 aims at observing and recognizing main groups of animals, plants, and microorganisms.

ID: G5
  - entity_id: G5
  - entity_type: major
  - description: G5 là một Mục tiêu Học tập (CLO) toàn diện với nhiều khía cạnh khác nhau, phù hợp với các lĩnh vực kỹ thuật đa dạng và phức tạp. Nó bao gồm mục tiêu học tập liên quan đến mạng máy tính, như giao thức Internet, thiết bị mạng, vấn đề an ninh mạng và sự cố kết nối.  Đối với lập trình và phát triển phần mềm, G5 yêu cầu sinh viên giải thích và trình bày các khái niệm lập trình bằng tiếng Anh, thiết kế giao diện web tĩnh sử dụng HTML và CSS, và sử dụng công cụ quản lý dự án phần mềm và quy trình. Mục tiêu này cũng liên quan đến kỹ năng quản lý dự án thực hành.  Trong lĩnh vực dữ liệu, G5 chuẩn bị sinh viên cho nghiên cứu sâu về phân tích dữ liệu lớn bằng cách biểu diễn dưới dạng đồ thị. Ngoài ra, nó yêu cầu sinh viên giải thích cách hoạt động của các thuật toán cơ bản trong khai thác dữ liệu. Tổng hợp lại, G5 định hướng phát triển kỹ năng chuyên môn kỹ thuật, khả năng nghiên cứu phân tích sâu và kỹ năng quản lý dự án.  G5 cũng bao gồm mục tiêu học tập liên quan đến an ninh mạng, hiểu và áp dụng các cơ chế bảo mật khác nhau ở cấp độ cơ bản. Ngoài ra, nó yêu cầu thực hành viết các bài báo hoặc báo cáo khoa học công nghệ.  Mục tiêu này còn liên quan đến việc sử dụng các công nghệ mở rộng trong những thực践 tốt nhất cho các vấn đề ảnh và video.  G5 cũng bao gồm mục tiêu để nhận dạng đồ họa 3D trong bối cảnh học thuật và ngành công nghiệp. Ngoài ra, G5 tập trung vào việc hiểu các khái niệm cơ bản của sinh thái học.  Cuối cùng, G5 liên quan đến việc sử dụng thiết bị cơ bản và dụng cụ trong một laborator y học chung.

  - entity_id: G6

  - description: G6 is a Course Learning Objective (CLO) that encompasses a range of skills and knowledge areas related to software development, artificial intelligence, and professional practice. It outlines specific learning goals for students, including the development of core algorithms for applications in navigation, pathfinding, and process optimization. Furthermore, G6 focuses on key professional and teamwork competencies. They must also explain standards related to the software development process. An overarching aspect of G6 is its aim to help students overcome psychological inertia, fostering an adaptive and innovative mindset. ID: Examination Forms
  - entity_id: Examination Forms
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Examination Forms refer to the diverse and non-standardized methods of assessment used to evaluate student performance within specific academic courses. These forms are defined per course and serve as an umbrella term for all assessment instruments, encompassing a broad range of written, practical, project-based, and continuous evaluations.  A core list of Examination Forms can be synthesized from common descriptions. Traditional timed written examinations include the Final Examination, Mid-term Examination, Close Test, and Open Test. Continuous and smaller-scale assessments comprise Homework, Exercises, quizzes, quick tests, and in-class activities. Practical and laboratory components are represented by Lab mid-term exams and Lab final exams. Project-based and demonstrative assessments form a significant category, including Lab Reports, Written Reports, Research Projects, Case Studies, Essays, Individual Presentations, and Group Presentations.  The specific combination of forms varies significantly by course. For instance, a technical course may utilize Final and Mid-term Examinations alongside Lab exams and Homework. Project-oriented courses, such as those in research or design, might emphasize Research Projects, Lab Reports, and Presentations, while other courses may incorporate a wider array of forms like Open Tests, Quizzes, and Case Studies. This illustrates that Examination Forms collectively represent all assessment instruments, from high-stakes exams to ongoing quizzes and project deliverables.<SEP>Examination Forms for the course include Close Test, Final Examination, Group Presentation, Research Project, Case Study, and Exercise.<SEP>Examination Forms refer to the various methods of assessment used in the course, including Close Test, Final Exam, Multiple Choice, Exercise, and Mid-term Exam.

ID: Class Attendance
  - entity_id: Class Attendance
  - entity_type: other
  - description: Class Attendance refers to the regular, punctual, and timely physical presence of students in their scheduled class sessions. It is a fundamental academic requirement and a core component of institutional policies, consistently and strongly advised as necessary for students to fully grasp and understand the concepts taught in a course.  Educational institutions reinforce its critical importance for academic success by incorporating it into academic schedules and establishing specific policy requirements. These policies often impose penalties for excessive absence, such as for missing more than three theory sessions, underscoring the view that consistent attendance is key to a student's ability to engage with and master the course material. ID: E-mail
  - entity_id: E-mail
  - entity_type: artifact
  - description: E-mail là một công cụ giao tiếp được sử dụng trong bối cảnh học tập, đặc biệt là trong các khóa học như Robot Vision. Mục đích chính của e-mail là để facilitates liên lạc giữa giảng viên và sinh viên, cũng như việc truyền đạt tài liệu học tập và bài tập. Cả sinh viên và giảng viên đều có thể sử dụng email cho mục đích này, với thông tin được truyền đi trong cả hai chiều.  E-mail thường được sử dụng nhất quán trong suốt thời gian của khóa học để duy trì liên lạc tiếp tục và trao đổi tài liệu. Ngoài ra, nó còn được sử dụng để cập nhật các thông báo quan trọng từ giảng viên đến sinh viên.   Các yêu cầu về e-mail cũng bao gồm việc kiểm tra email hàng ngày bởi sinh viên để nhận được các thông tin từ giảng viên.

  - description: **Tóm tắt danh sách đọc**  Danh sách đọc (Reading List) cho khóa học CSC10012, viết bởi Phan Dung, là một compilation chi tiết của cả các tài liệu bắt buộc và bổ sung nhằm hỗ trợ mục tiêu học tập của khóa học. Compilation này bao gồm rất nhiều tài liệu về kỹ thuật đại cương và chuyên sâu, đáp ứng nhu cầu thông tin đa dạng.  **Tài liệu bắt buộc**  Danh sách bắt buộc chứa một số cuốn sách quan trọng như "Mathematics for Machine Learning" bởi Marc Peter Deisenroth, A. Aldo Faisal, và Cheng Soon Ong; "Modern Database System, 12th Edition"; và "Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools". Ngoài ra còn có các tài liệu liên quan đến thiết kế người dùng như "Don't Make Me Think, Revisited" và "Sketching User Experiences", cũng như các tác phẩm cơ bản về kỹ thuật phần mềm, UML, phân tích và thiết kế hướng đối tượng (OOAD), và lập trình dựa trên mô hình (MDE). Tài liệu còn bao gồm các cuốn sách khác về quản trị kinh doanh, quản lý quy trình kinh doanh, thông tin kinh doanh và phân tích dữ liệu, như "Business Intelligence, Analytics, and Data Science" và "The Enterprise Big Data Lake". Ngoài ra còn có những cuốn sách về hình học phẳng, xử lý tín hiệu số, bảo mật thông tin, và học máy. Các cuốn sách cần thiết khác bao gồm "Thống Kê Máy Tính", "Digital Image Processing 4th Edition", "Learning Image Processing with OpenCV", "Computer Vision: A Modern Approach (2nd Edition)", "Probabilistic Robotics", "Computer Vision: Algorithms and Applications", "Dive into Deep Learning", "Interactive Computer Graphics: A Top-Down Approach with WebGL 7th Edition", và "Graph Data Mining: Algorithm, Security and Application".  **Tài liệu bổ sung**  Một số tài liệu bổ sung khác có thể bao gồm các cuốn sách về trực quan hóa dữ liệu như "Better Data Visualizations: A Guide for Scholars, Researchers, and Wonks"; kỹ thuật Hadoop và Spark; và các chủ đề tiên tiến như blockchain và học sâu áp dụng. Tài liệu này cũng bao gồm những cuốn sách cơ bản về tư duy và suy luận, hai cuốn sách tiếng Việt "Khởi nghiệp tinh gọn" và "Khởi nghiệp thông minh"; hướng dẫn viết ngữ pháp và bài viết nghiên cứu; và cuốn sách "An Introduction to Statistical Learning".   - description: Semester 6 is an academic term within its program structure, designated for a variety of core and specialized courses spanning several key areas of study.   - description: Final Examination is a standard and major form of academic assessment used to evaluate student performance at the conclusion of a university course or academic term. It serves as a culminating and formal evaluation of a student's understanding of the course material and acquired competencies, typically constituting a significant component of a course's overall grade. As a primary examination form within comprehensive evaluation schemes, it is employed across a wide variety of disciplines.  Specific examples of courses that utilize a Final Examination include MTH00053, CSC10105, Data Visualization, CSC12109, CSC12107, Applied Data Analytics, CSC14003, CSC15007, Compiler, Introduction to Big Data, Computer Vision, Robot Vision, CSC16109, Recommender Systems, Programming for Data Science, Graph Mining, and Environmental Science.   - description: Learning Objectives, often abbreviated as CLOs (Course Learning Objectives), are the specific goals or intended learning outcomes that students are expected to achieve upon successful completion of a course. They serve as a clear statement of the skills, knowledge, and competencies students will attain, thereby guiding course structure and providing a measure for achievement. These objectives are sometimes enumerated, for example as goals G1 through G6.  The scope of Learning Objectives is broad, covering foundational to higher-order competencies across various disciplines. They typically encompass understanding fundamental concepts, theories, and key terminology within a field, such as basic DSP theory, discrete time systems, Computer Security risks, or managerial ethics. Students are expected to explain core concepts, statistics, and computer science terminologies clearly.  A central aim is the application of knowledge to practical, professional, and analytical scenarios. This includes applying classroom learning to optimize programs (e.g., using CUDA), utilizing professional tools, applying cryptographic techniques, security mechanisms, and statistical or machine learning models to real-world problems. Students also engage in analyzing complex systems and problems, evaluating outcomes, and understanding specific processes like startup development.  Learning Objectives strongly emphasize the development of professional and collaborative skills. These include applying teamwork, establishing effective teams, practicing group work, and delivering presentations. They also involve technical communication, such as writing comprehensive reports, practicing technical documentation, and defending project work.  Finally, CLOs often define the creation of tangible outputs and engagement with practical engineering disciplines. Students are expected to build software products, create prototypes, propose development models, and operate common development tasks. The objectives ensure students can read relevant literature, apply engineering standards, and adapt to a professional working environment.

ID: Trần Đan Thư
  - entity_id: Trần Đan Thư
  - entity_type: major
  - description: Trần Đan Thư is a Vietnamese author of several textbooks and recommended reading materials in the fields of computer science and information security. He is an author of fundamental programming textbooks, including "Nhập môn lập trình" (Introduction to Programming) and "Kỹ thuật lập trình" (Programming Techniques), as well as a text on "Lập trình hướng đối tượng" (Object-Oriented Programming).  A significant focus of his work is on the theoretical foundations of information security. He is a co-author of the textbook "Cơ Sở Lí Thuyết Số Trong An Toàn - Bảo Mật Thông Tin" (The Foundation of Number Theory in Information Security). This work, also referenced under similar titles such as "Cơ sở Lý thuyết số cho Bảo mật thông tin" and a version specifying "phần mã công khai" (public key part), serves as both required and recommended reading for academic courses. His materials are consistently listed as essential resources for university-level instruction.

ID: University
  - entity_id: University
  - entity_type: organization
  - description: Trường đại học là một cơ sở giáo dục cao cấp quản lý các trường và chính sách đào tạo. Trường đại học chịu trách nhiệm lập, thực thi và kiểm tra các chính sách giáo dục mà sinh viên cần tuân theo. Một yếu tố quan trọng trong việc điều hành này là việc đảm bảo tính integrità học thuật của sinh viên. Trường đại học quản lý hồ sơ đăng ký thi và tiến hành cuộc xem xét kỷ luật cho những vi phạm liên quan đến integrità học thuật, bao gồm truy cập trái phép vào bài kiểm tra và sao chép văn bản khác.  Trường đại học là nơi mà các chính sách và quy tắc được áp dụng chặt chẽ. Các vi phạm như sao chép trong kỳ thi hoặc lạm dụng quyền lợi của người khác đều phải được báo cáo cho trường đại học để thực hiện cuộc xem xét, giải quyết và đánh giá theo quy trình của trường.  Việc tuân thủ các chính sách và quy tắc là một yêu cầu quan trọng đối với sinh viên. Trường đại học đảm bảo rằng tất cả các sinh viên hiểu rõ những điều họ cần làm trong môi trường giáo dục cao cấp của mình.

ID: Pearson
  - entity_id: Pearson
  - entity_type: organization
  - description: Pearson is a major international publishing company specializing in educational materials, with a particularly significant role in higher education. It is frequently cited as the publisher of primary, required, or widely used textbooks across a diverse range of academic and professional disciplines.  In the fields of computer science and information technology, Pearson publishes foundational texts such as "Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach," "Computer Vision: A Modern Approach (2nd Edition)," and "Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach (8th Edition)." Its catalog includes key programming textbooks like "Programming: Principles and Practice Using C++ (2nd Edition)" and "Absolute C++ (6th Edition)," as well as algorithm texts including "Analysis of Algorithms (3rd edition)" and "Introduction to the Design and Analysis of Algorithms (3rd edition)."  Pearson also publishes extensively in data-centric and analytical disciplines. Its offerings include database publications like "Database Systems: The Complete Book," data mining texts such as "Introduction to Data Mining, 2nd Edition," and managerial perspectives like "Business Intelligence, Analytics, and Data Science: A Managerial Perspective" and "Analytics, Data Science, & Artificial Intelligence: Systems for Decision Support." ID: Theory Sessions
  - entity_id: Theory Sessions
  - entity_type: other
  - description: Theory Sessions are the scheduled lecture or instructional periods for a course, specifically identified as a fundamental component of the Career Observation class structure. These class meetings are focused on delivering theoretical instruction and teaching the core concepts and foundational knowledge of the subject matter.  A defining and strict feature of these sessions is their mandatory attendance policy. The policy consistently states that a student's attendance is a prerequisite for examination eligibility. Specifically, if a student is absent from more than three Theory Sessions, they are disqualified or barred from taking the course examinations; this is uniformly described as prohibiting or not permitting exam participation.<SEP>Theory sessions refer to lectures or classes taught by instructors.

ID: Exams
  - entity_id: Exams
  - entity_type: event
  - description: Exams are the formal assessments for the Career Observation course, constituting a required part of the course's overall evaluation. Students are generally allowed to participate in these exams, but their eligibility is governed by a strict and consistent attendance policy. This policy specifically bars a student from taking the exams if they are absent for more than three of the course's theory sessions. The rule is applied regardless of the reason for the absences. Therefore, exams are assessments from which students may be barred if they exceed this allowed number of absences.<SEP>Exams are assessments that students who miss more than three theory sessions are barred from taking.

ID: Cheating
  - entity_id: Cheating
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Cheating is an academic violation, specifically a form of academic dishonesty or misconduct, that is strictly prohibited. The primary and consistent consequence for a student found to have committed cheating is receiving a grade of zero for the course in question. Beyond this academic penalty, the incident triggers an official review process. Standard procedure involves reporting the violation to relevant school and university authorities for further investigation and potential disciplinary action.<SEP>Cheating is an academic violation for which students will receive a grade of 0 for the course.<SEP>Cheating is an academic violation that results in a grade of zero and a report to school and university authorities.<SEP>Cheating is an academic violation that results in a grade of zero for the course and further review by the school and university.

ID: Plagiarism
  - entity_id: Plagiarism
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Plagiarism is an act of academic dishonesty and a specific form of academic misconduct or integrity violation. It is defined as using another person's work without providing proper credit or attribution. The consistent and primary academic consequence for committing plagiarism is that the student receives a grade of zero for the course in which the violation occurred.  This penalty triggers further institutional processes. The act is subject to a formal disciplinary review and is reported to relevant authorities. Specifically, the incident is reported both to the school—typically the specific academic department or faculty involved—and to broader university authorities for further evaluation and potential action beyond the academic grade penalty.<SEP>Plagiarism is an academic violation for which students will receive a grade of 0 for the course.<SEP>Plagiarism is an academic violation that results in a grade of zero and a report to school and university authorities.<SEP>Plagiarism is an academic violation that results in a grade of zero for the course and further review by the school and university.

ID: Class Discussions

  - description: Class Discussions are interactive forums, conversations, or sessions held during class meetings that serve as a fundamental component of course participation. Their primary purpose is to deepen the collective understanding of the subject matter and to foster a collaborative learning environment. ID: Learning Objectives (CLOs)
  - entity_id: Learning Objectives (CLOs)
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Learning Objectives (CLOs) are specific, stated goals or competencies that students are expected to achieve upon completing a course. They serve as a comprehensive framework for student learning outcomes, particularly within fields like information technology and computer science. The objectives consistently emphasize a blend of technical proficiency, analytical ability, professional soft skills, and foundational knowledge.  Technically, CLOs require students to describe and use core programming concepts, implement data structures, write programs, and apply software development modeling. Database competencies include applying Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) principles, operating relational databases, and engaging in database design. Analytical skills involve analyzing problems, conducting data analysis, evaluating algorithm complexity, applying analytical methods, synthesizing test specifications, and executing data analysis steps. Students are also expected to develop capabilities in designing systems, such as for image or video processing, and to propose solutions and develop new ideas.  A strong focus is placed on communication, documentation, and contextual understanding. Objectives require students to explain technical concepts and models in English, understand English terminology and documents, produce models and reports, defend projects, practice writing technical reports, and use technical documents effectively. Broader contextual knowledge includes explaining enterprise information systems, discussing types of information systems, describing system development challenges, recognizing information technology's role in business, and understanding concepts like differential privacy measures and mechanisms.  Collaborative, managerial, and personal skills form another core component. CLOs highlight the need to demonstrate teamwork, practice management within teams, apply personal and teamwork skills, work both individually and in groups, and recognize specific roles in group activities. Foundational cognitive skills, such as critical thinking, are integral to these comprehensive learning outcomes.<SEP>Learning Objectives (CLOs) define the skills students will gain, including reading English materials, expressing issues, critical thinking, and technology implementation.<SEP>Learning Objectives (CLOs) are specific goals students must achieve upon completing the Graph Mining course, such as constructing reports and explaining concepts.

  - description: Individual Work đề cập đến các bài tập học thuật hoặc công việc cá nhân là một phần không thể thiếu của chương trình học. Nguyên tắc cơ bản là mỗi sinh viên phải hoàn thành và nộp bài làm độc lập, đảm bảo sản phẩm cuối cùng phản ánh nỗ lực riêng tư của họ. Mục đích chính là đánh giá và đánh giá năng lực, hiểu biết và hiệu suất cá nhân của từng sinh viên.  Trong khi các hoạt động hợp tác như nhóm nghiên cứu hoặc thảo luận nhóm được khuyến khích để học hỏi, trao đổi ý tưởng và hỗ trợ chung cho việc học, điều này không làm thay đổi yêu cầu cơ bản về hoàn thành độc lập và nộp bài. Do đó, dù có tham gia trong những môi trường hợp tác như vậy, nội dung công việc vẫn phải được thực hiện và nộp bởi mỗi sinh viên cá nhân.  Bài làm cá nhân yêu cầu phải được hoàn thành và nộp bởi từng sinh viên một cách riêng lẻ, ngay cả khi khuyến khích thảo luận nhóm. Trong một số trường hợp, các hoạt động nhóm có thể được tích hợp vào quá trình học tập để tăng cường trao đổi ý tưởng và hỗ trợ, nhưng điều quan trọng là mỗi người vẫn phải chịu trách nhiệm hoàn thành và nộp bài làm của riêng mình.

  - description: Homework is a formal examination and assessment form used in university courses, particularly within computer science and related technical fields. It consists of assignments given to students to be completed independently outside of regular class time. Its primary functions are to evaluate student performance, assess understanding and progress, provide practical application of concepts, and serve as required work that forms an integral part of a course's overall evaluation structure.  This entity is explicitly identified as a core examination component in numerous specific courses. These include CSC10012, CSC10105, CSC12107, CSC14003, CSC16109, the Applied Data Analytics course, the Compiler course, the Computer Security course, the Computer Vision course, the Robot Vision course, and the Programming for Data Science course. Within these contexts, homework functions alongside other assessment methods such as final examinations and research projects. The consistent characteristic of homework across all descriptions is its dual role as assigned tasks for external completion, used for both student practice and formal academic assessment.<SEP>An examination form consisting of assigned work.<SEP>Homework assignments are given to students to reinforce their learning and understanding of course concepts.

ID: Research Project
  - entity_id: Research Project
  - entity_type: method
  - description: A Research Project is an examination form and assignment type used within various university courses to assess student performance. It is one of several possible assessment methods in a course structure, often serving as a final project. The primary purpose is to evaluate student performance through research-based work, requiring students to conduct an in-depth investigation on a course-related topic. This involves substantive research and the application of course concepts to a practical or investigative task, which can include exploring new software technologies or other subject-specific topics, with students often expected to present their findings.  Specifically, the Research Project has been identified as an assessment component in multiple courses, including CSC13002, CSC14003, CSC16109, Compiler, Computer Vision, Robot Vision, and Recommender Systems. It is a key output of these courses, designed to assess students through a project that demands research.<SEP>An examination form for the Programming for Data Science course.<SEP>Research project is an examination form involving in-depth study on a course topic.

ID: Cheating And Plagiarism
  - entity_id: Cheating And Plagiarism
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Cheating and Plagiarism constitute prohibited acts of academic dishonesty and formal academic misconduct, violating standards of academic integrity. According to institutional and course policies, any student found responsible for either cheating or plagiarism will receive an academic penalty of a grade of zero for the course in which the violation occurred.  This incident is not handled solely by the course instructor. The violation must be formally reported and submitted to the relevant school and university authorities. This mandatory reporting initiates a further disciplinary review process by the institution, which may lead to additional sanctions beyond the academic penalty for the course.<SEP>Cheating and plagiarism are academic violations that result in a grade of zero for the course and a report to the school and university.
  - source_id: chunk-971be220bb86f5aa14877b4902bab258<SEP>chunk-666ad22d606069331e8110161c52ce2e<SEP>chunk-a7f71d80abc6083184932aa30b343bb1<SEP>chunk-9a29c759ae99046ac2c3fe8bdf371df1<SEP>chunk-e3da7d39b6bdf31797321b628d538fae<SEP>chunk-29f2f81143ba1b329cf62c5e0e5a103c<SEP>chunk-2d12367f0ebb6a75ee23ea936d23092c<SEP>chunk-a57bed8cbf2444e09e5c94a70408bb2f<SEP>chunk-5c15d3dc15c83a9ad079fa6d593c5a10<SEP>chunk-dbadcb2ddf3d27bcf6666e73868b9263<SEP>chunk-316f9be9c7846e523d2eea43d4923668<SEP>chunk-46e0cea33d8bc19415cb42e98e486b17<SEP>chunk-652de78190ebf0514cebc565dd40a2b1<SEP>chunk-07be09f88fdef9e0115c48cbd0461f2e<SEP>chunk-9d47124df16dc180dc9081323ef6969c<SEP>chunk-8349910d2f3e3207a3f720b885c1be9e<SEP>chunk-aa300ca652ea56cbe3bd66f69f239612<SEP>chunk-fe7b66583e0ebcf99a327b80a554972b<SEP>chunk-a20a9abadf6e6db8ee68045a4fa77d97<SEP>chunk-5001b9b9270de6882a183e70b7e2d03f<SEP>chunk-2ea14fbb3d356468c4c38db5ff86f44a<SEP>chunk-33b50d807bc6b3e3a8fa7b788b291716<SEP>chunk-7b3d4a2b4bfd5ebe502d4d78f868027b<SEP>chunk-dc817b239ade09690d81ceb8681bcac6<SEP>chunk-b4f0f8890cd634d82861e9f6b1e4d651<SEP>chunk-6a3ef3ae3ef8f5756ce93bb25a979566<SEP>chunk-56b909a80e97ccea54aceb2a12e7205d<SEP>chunk-9280127bdfa6c6d5e487dad0281a1017<SEP>chunk-d1c8d5fa6ea7d2fcff1f219710ad4e10<SEP>chunk-a0fdb618d5a1584e6d38156e8a6a5c85<SEP>chunk-f97280a6c8b34a5e9c7d8c2110c4c55c<SEP>chunk-b091fcf422a93cf36c409ab4818cbdf6<SEP>chunk-e79e14eb00fdc0c4eba369cfbab55075<SEP>chunk-24d27908a5a788a8b6d92194c3a7894b<SEP>chunk-854c1b2973e9f64de68020a004cc4eab<SEP>chunk-0ad54f9fdb71854036b09b8edbdd8219<SEP>chunk-fa37b30e4078ae1165deddafc4d0b7a6<SEP>chunk-7b3911deed5991e64416fd3307a4a966<SEP>chunk-fe50ab5062233febc53947436885ba4a<SEP>chunk-53f341e155fc927ef49618789657b445<SEP>chunk-8579a62ce97a2889f39f57e50661604d<SEP>chunk-c5c58542f250303c363c3bf141a3405f<SEP>chunk-ca04808a223cdacb67451940ce5904a1<SEP>chunk-d8dbc93c6aebfa8acd8912513f6f4bd8

ID: School And University Regulations
  - entity_id: School And University Regulations
  - entity_type: content
  - description: School and University Regulations are the official, overarching rules and policies established by an educational institution to govern its operations. They constitute the formal framework for student conduct, academic procedures, and institutional management. A fundamental requirement for all students is to read, understand, and follow these regulations strictly. This obligation is a core part of general student responsibility and is often explicitly mandated within specific course contexts and policies.  These regulations cover key areas such as attendance requirements, academic integrity standards, and rules for general conduct. In essence, they represent the complete set of official institutional policies with which every student must comply. All students are therefore accountable for adhering to the regulations and policies of their specific school or university.<SEP>School and university regulations are policies that all students must read and follow strictly.<SEP>School And University Regulations are the policies that all students are responsible for reading and following strictly.

ID: Instructor
  - entity_id: Instructor
  - entity_type: person
  - description: The Instructor is the primary teacher and authority figure for a given course, responsible for its overall delivery, management, and associated policies. Their core duties include setting participation expectations, establishing and enforcing all course policies, and disseminating course materials. A central aspect of their role is to serve as the main point of contact for student communication.  This communication occurs primarily through two channels: email and the Moodle learning platform. A specific and consistently emphasized protocol requires that all student emails must use a subject line formatted to include the Course ID, ensuring proper identification. The Instructor is designated as the primary recipient for all correspondence adhering to this policy. In this capacity, they receive, read, and respond to student emails. Therefore, students should direct their course-related emails to the Instructor, provided the required subject format is used.<SEP>The Instructor is the teacher of the course who receives emails from students and is referenced in communication protocols.<SEP>The Instructor is the recipient of emails from students, which must include the Course ID in the subject line.<SEP>Instructor is the teacher of the course whom students must contact via email using a specific subject format.<SEP>The instructor will communicate with students via Moodle and e-mail.<SEP>The instructor will receive emails from students, which should follow a specific format.<SEP>The instructor supervises and interacts with students via email, according to the course requirements.

ID: Study And Examination Requirements
  - entity_id: Study And Examination Requirements
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Study and Examination Requirements are the formal rules and policies that students must follow within a course, such as an Earth Science course. These requirements govern student conduct, assessment, and the maintenance of academic integrity. They specifically outline expectations and stipulations for attendance, class participation, collaborative or group work, and the use of official communication protocols, typically conducted through designated platforms like Moodle and email.  The requirements establish clear consequences for violations of academic integrity, enforcing penalties for cheating and plagiarism. They also detail policies regarding the encouragement of study groups and the proper flow of information between students and instructors. Overall, these requirements serve as a comprehensive set of guidelines covering attendance, plagiarism, group work, participation, and communication for the course.

ID: Class Session
  - entity_id: Class Session
  - entity_type: event
  - description: A Class Session is a scheduled meeting for a course, serving as the primary setting for instruction. It is a meeting where course materials are presented and covered, often involving discussions and activities, and students are expected to attend on time and participate.  A consistent and critical policy is that if a student misses a class session, they are solely responsible for the content that was covered. This responsibility explicitly includes the obligation to find out what materials, information, or discussions they missed and to catch up on that work independently.<SEP>A class session is an event where course materials are covered, and students are expected to be on time and active.<SEP>A class session is a scheduled meeting where course materials are covered.<SEP>A scheduled meeting of the course; students are responsible for catching up on missed material if absent.<SEP>A scheduled meeting of the course where materials are covered and discussions take place.<SEP>A class session is a scheduled meeting where course materials are covered.<SEP>A Class Session is a scheduled meeting; if missed, the student is responsible for finding out the covered materials.

ID: Course Id
  - entity_id: Course Id
  - entity_type: courseid
  - description: Course Id is a unique identifier code for a specific course, such as CSC10001. Its primary function is to facilitate and categorize communication with the instructor by ensuring the proper identification and routing of course-related emails.  To achieve this, the Course Id must be included within the subject line of any email sent to the instructor. The required format for this inclusion is to enclose the code within square brackets, as exemplified by "[CSC10001]". In this communication protocol, the code itself acts as the unique key that identifies the course.<SEP>Course Id is a code used to identify the course, such as CSC10001, and must be included in email subjects.<SEP>A specific identifier for a course, used in email subject lines for communication with the instructor.<SEP>The Course ID is a specific identifier that must be included in the subject line of emails sent to the instructor, for example "[CSC10001]".<SEP>Course Id is a specific identifier for the course, required in the subject line of emails to the instructor, e.g., "[CSC10001]".

ID: Academic Integrity Policy
  - entity_id: Academic Integrity Policy
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: The Academic Integrity Policy is a formal regulation that addresses academic misconduct, specifically cheating and plagiarism. The policy states that any student found to have engaged in such acts will receive a grade of 0 for the course in which the violation occurred. Furthermore, the incident triggers an official review process. This review is typically conducted by the relevant school or department and may also involve a higher-level review by the university administration. The consistent purpose of the policy is to uphold academic standards by defining a clear and significant consequence for violations and ensuring a structured follow-up procedure.<SEP>The Academic Integrity Policy states that cheating or plagiarism results in a grade of 0 for the course and further disciplinary review.

  - description: Semester 5 is an academic term within a university curriculum, specifically serving as the designated period for teaching a variety of core and advanced modules. It is a pivotal point, typically in the latter half of an undergraduate program, where students engage with subject matter that builds upon foundational knowledge. The semester's curriculum is characterized by a diverse course load, primarily in the fields of computer science and information technology.  Key modules taught during Semester 5 include CSC10105, Advanced Computer Networking, Database Management Systems, CSC12109, CSC13002, CSC14003, Digital Image and Video Processing, and specialized courses such as "Phát triển ứng dụng web" (Web Application Development), Data Mining and Applications, and Introduction to Cryptography. ID: Personal Experiences
  - entity_id: Personal Experiences
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Personal Experiences, within an educational context, refer to the individual life events, knowledge, or professional backgrounds of students. These experiences are actively encouraged by educators to be related to and integrated with the formal class material. The primary purpose of this practice is to enrich classroom discussions, allowing students to connect theoretical concepts to their own real-world understanding. This pedagogical approach expects students to draw upon their unique histories and insights to deepen their engagement with and comprehension of the subject matter.

ID: Assignments
  - entity_id: Assignments
  - entity_type: artifact
  - description: Các giao đề (assignments) là các nhiệm vụ hoặc công việc được phân phối cho học sinh như một phần của yêu cầu khóa học. Chúng được phát đi qua nền tảng Moodle và email, với yêu cầu học sinh phải hoàn thành và nộp chúng riêng lẻ. Các nhiệm vụ này xuất hiện xuyên suốt quá trình học và liên quan trực tiếp đến tiến độ và đánh giá học tập của từng học viên.

ID: Class Participation
  - entity_id: Class Participation
  - entity_type: other
  - description: **Class Participation**  Class Participation trong giáo dục là mong đợi về sự tham gia tích cực và liên quan của học sinh vào các buổi giảng dạy. Mục đích này không chỉ bao gồm việc đi đến lớp mà còn yêu cầu sự góp mặt thường xuyên và ý nghĩa cho quá trình học. Các hành vi cơ bản thể hiện sự tham gia này bao gồm đóng góp vào cuộc thảo luận nhóm, chia sẻ suy nghĩ cá nhân và phân tích, và đặt câu hỏi liên quan.  Các yếu tố quan trọng và được nhấn mạnh thường xuyên trong class participation là khả năng liên kết nội dung khóa học với kinh nghiệm cá nhân, các ngữ cảnh bên ngoài hoặc các chủ đề học thuật khác. Việc này giúp làm sâu sắc quá trình học không chỉ cho sinh viên mà còn cho đồng nghiệp của họ. Tóm lại, class participation được định nghĩa như một phương pháp tiêu chuẩn để tạo sự tham gia của học sinh trong lớp học.  Class Participation liên quan đến việc đóng góp vào cuộc thảo luận và các hoạt động khác, cung cấp suy nghĩ, đặt câu hỏi và liên kết nội dung với kinh nghiệm cá nhân và bên ngoài.<SEP>Active participation includes contributing to discussions and relating material to personal experiences.

ID: Self-Study Hours
  - entity_id: Self-Study Hours
  - entity_type: content
  - description: Self-Study Hours is a term used in university course descriptions to denote the amount of time allocated for independent student work outside of formal instruction. It consistently refers to approximately 116.25 hours. This time is designated for a combination of activities, primarily including self-study, independent study, and examination preparation. Descriptions also note that this allocation encompasses "other activities," integrating it into the broader total course workload.  The term has been applied as a standard metric for planning student effort in various subjects within the curriculum. Specific courses mentioned include Data Visualization, User Interface Design, and those coded CSC13002 and CSC16109. Its consistent definition across these courses indicates it is a formal component used to structure and communicate expected student effort.<SEP>Self-study hours represent approximately 116.25 hours for preparation and other activities for the Graph Mining course.<SEP>Self-Study Hours refers to the approximately 57.5 hours allocated for self-study, examination preparation, and other activities in the Environmental Science course workload.<SEP>The course includes ~112.5 hours of self-study time.

ID: School Regulations
  - entity_id: School Regulations
  - entity_type: policy
  - description: School Regulations are the official rules and policies established by an educational institution, such as a school or university, that all enrolled students are responsible for reading, understanding, and following. These regulations constitute a mandatory framework for student conduct and academic life. Adherence to these policies is required strictly, often as a fundamental condition of enrollment and participation in courses. The purpose of School Regulations is to provide a consistent set of guidelines that govern behavior, academic integrity, and institutional procedures within the educational environment.<SEP>School regulations are policies that all students are responsible for reading and following strictly.<SEP>School regulations are policies that all students are responsible for reading and following strictly.<SEP>School Regulations are policies that students must read and follow strictly.<SEP>The School Regulations outline the rules and policies that all students must follow at the school.

ID: Semester 7
  - entity_id: Semester 7
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Semester 7 is a key academic semester in its associated curriculum, characterized by a strong focus on advanced, project-based, and professional preparation courses. It serves as the designated period for several major culminating projects, including the Capstone Projects course, the Undergraduate Thesis, and the Industrial-based Thesis.  The semester covers a comprehensive range of advanced technical and specialized subjects. Core software engineering courses include Software Project Management, Software Architecture, and Software Modeling. Critical technical modules taught are Computer Security, Digital Signal Processing, User Interface Design, Applied Data Analytics, Introduction to Data Science, and Blockchain and Applications. Specific course codes offered during this term are CSC12107 and CSC14117.<SEP>Semester 7 is the semester in which the course is taught.<SEP>Semester 7 is the academic semester in which the course is taught.<SEP>The semester in which the course is taught.<SEP>The semester in which the CSC16109 course is taught.<SEP>The semester in which the course is taught.

ID: Teamwork Skills
  - entity_id: Teamwork Skills
  - entity_type: concept
  - description: Teamwork Skills are a defined learning objective, specifically referenced as G1, within an academic course context. The primary purpose of this objective is for students to develop and apply collaborative abilities to complete various course requirements effectively. Students practice these skills by working together in groups on assignments, presentations, reports, and a final project. The application of Teamwork Skills involves several specific academic tasks: presenting topics related to Data Warehousing (DW) and Business Intelligence (BI), designing user interfaces collaboratively, and investigating problems within the field of data science. While most descriptions are consistent, one note specifies the Environmental Science course as the context, suggesting the learning objective G1 may be a transferable or standardized component applied across different courses or disciplines with similar collaborative learning goals.

  - entity_id: O'Reilly Media

  - description: O'Reilly Media is a publisher specializing in technical and professional books, particularly in the fields of software development, web technologies, data science, and project management. The company publishes a wide range of textbooks and guides for both beginners and experienced practitioners.  In the domain of web and JavaScript development, O'Reilly Media publishes titles such as "Learning Web Design: A Beginner's Guide to HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and Web Graphics," "Web Development with Node and Express," "Learning React Native," and "Learning React." For data engineering and big data technologies, it is the publisher of authoritative works including "Hadoop: The Definitive Guide," "Learning Spark: Lightning-Fast Data Analytics," and "Spark: The Definitive Guide."  The publisher's catalog also extends to software architecture, project management, blockchain, and programming languages. Key titles in these areas include "Fundamentals of Software Architecture: An Engineering Approach," "Making Things Happen: Mastering Project Management," "Mastering Blockchain: Unlocking the Power of Cryptocurrencies, Smart Contracts, and Decentralized Applications," and "Effective Modern C++: 42 Specific Ways to Improve Your Use of C++11 and C++14." Relation: Moodle -> Email
  - weight: 23.0
  - description: Quan hệ giữa Email và Moodle được mô tả như sự kết nối và bổ sung lẫn nhau của hai công cụ chính trong quá trình giao tiếp và truyền đạt thông tin trong môi trường giáo dục. Hệ thống đôi kênh này đảm bảo rằng thông tin cần thiết được truyền đạt hiệu quả cho sinh viên suốt quá trình học. Email đóng vai trò trong việc gửi thông báo nhanh chóng và trực tiếp đến sinh viên. Relation: Course ID -> Email
  - weight: 16.0
  - description: The relation **(Course ID, Email)** describes a mandatory communication protocol in an academic setting. This protocol requires that all emails sent by students to an instructor must include the unique Course ID in the email's subject line. The instruction is consistently framed as a requirement, with descriptions using terms like "must include" or "must begin with." A typical implementation is for the subject line to begin with the Course ID, often formatted within square brackets (e.g., "[CSC10001]").  The primary purpose of this rule is to ensure proper identification and organization of correspondence. By immediately tagging an email with the Course ID, the protocol allows the instructor to efficiently categorize, filter, and prioritize incoming messages related to a specific course. This practice facilitates clear communication and effective email management for both parties involved.<SEP>The subject line of emails must begin with the Course ID format [CSC10001].

Relation: Study Groups -> Students
  - weight: 33.0
  - description: The relation (Students, Study Groups) describes a common academic practice in which students are encouraged to form study groups. The primary purpose of these groups is to facilitate collaborative learning through the discussion of course topics, allowing students to share insights and clarify complex subject matter together to enhance their overall understanding.  This collaborative activity, however, is consistently paired with a crucial academic integrity boundary. While discussion within the group is encouraged, all individual assignments and final work must be completed and submitted independently by each student. The relation therefore balances the benefits of collective learning with the fundamental requirement for personal academic accountability, ensuring that collaborative discussion does not extend to the unauthorized sharing of individual work.<SEP>Students are encouraged to form study groups to discuss course topics.<SEP>Students are encouraged to form study groups to discuss topics, but individual work must be done and submitted on their own.<SEP>Students form study groups for discussions.<SEP>Students form study groups for discussion.

Relation: Class Attendance -> Students

  - description: The relation (Class Attendance, Students) describes the link between student attendance and their academic performance and engagement. Students are uniformly and strongly advised to attend classes regularly and on time. This regular attendance is consistently described as necessary for students to fully grasp many, if not all, of the course concepts, and is considered a critical, recommended practice for achieving comprehensive learning outcomes.   - weight: 9.0
  - description: The relation (Course, Reading List) describes the connection between an academic course and its associated reading list. The reading list is a defined and integral part of the course, provided to students as a compilation of required or recommended materials for study. Its primary purpose is to supply additional resources to aid in learning the course subject matter, which in this context is database systems.  The content of the reading list specifically includes required materials such as textbooks, with one explicitly named example being "Modern Database System." Beyond textbooks, the list may also encompass other specific books, publications, lecture notes, and frequently asked questions (FAQs) documents. These materials collectively form the core or supplementary resources that students are expected to engage with as part of the course.<SEP>The course has a reading list that includes specified materials for students.

The primary purpose of this relation is to facilitate communication, where students send inquiries and messages to the instructor, and the instructor receives these messages, responds to them, and disseminates course materials.  A critical and consistently required rule governing this relation is the mandatory formatting of the email subject line. Students must begin the subject line with the specific Course ID. This requirement is set by the instructor to ensure proper identification, organization, and timely handling of all course-related correspondence.<SEP>Email is used to communicate with the instructor.<SEP>The instructor also communicates via email, particularly for urgent matters or specific instructions.

  - weight: 16.0

A student's eligibility to take exams is contingent upon their attendance in theory sessions. The core stipulation is that students who are absent for more than three theory sessions are not allowed, or are prohibited, from taking the exams. Consequently, exams serve as formal assessments from which students can be barred based solely on their failure to meet this attendance requirement.<SEP>Students are not allowed to take exams if they are absent for more than 3 theory sessions.

Relation: Students -> Cheating And Plagiarism
  - weight: 15.0
  - description: The relation "Cheating And Plagiarism, Students" describes the academic consequences for students who engage in such misconduct. The consistent policy across all descriptions is that students who commit cheating or plagiarism will receive a grade of zero for the course or assignment in question, which is characterized as a severe academic penalty.  This penalty is not the sole consequence. The policy mandates that the incident is formally reported for further review. This review process is conducted by the school and/or the university administration. Therefore, the primary outcomes for students involved are a grade of zero and the formal submission of the case to academic authorities for potential additional disciplinary action.
  - keywords: academic integrity,academic penalty,academic violation,consequence,consequences,disciplinary action,misconduct consequences,penalty,prohibition

  - description: The relation (Instructor, Students) describes a formal communication protocol within an academic setting. Under this protocol, students are required to communicate with their instructor via email for academic purposes, which includes sending inquiries and receiving communications and disseminated materials from the instructor.  A central and mandatory rule of this protocol is the use of a specific subject line format for all such emails.   - weight: 20.0
  - description: The relation between **Students** and **Theory Sessions** is defined by a mandatory attendance policy with a specific quantitative rule and consequence for non-compliance. Consistently, the rule states that if a student is absent for more than three theory sessions, they are not permitted to take the exams. Relation: Students -> Cheating
  - weight: 12.0
  - description: The relation **(Cheating, Students)** describes the consequences for students who engage in academic dishonesty. The consistent outcome across all descriptions is that a student who cheats will receive a grade of zero for the course in which the infraction occurred. Beyond this academic penalty, the incident typically triggers an official institutional process. Most descriptions specify that the case will be reported, submitted for a formal review, or face further review by the relevant academic authorities. This indicates that the grade penalty is the immediate academic consequence, but it is often accompanied by an administrative procedure that may lead to additional disciplinary actions.<SEP>Students committing cheating will receive a grade of zero for the course and be reported to school and university authorities.<SEP>Students who engage in cheating will receive a grade of zero for the course.<SEP>Students who commit cheating will be graded 0 for the course.<SEP>For any kind of cheating, students will be graded 0 for the course and the incident is submitted for review.
  - keywords: academic integrity,academic violation,disciplinary action,grading,grading consequence,penalty,violation,violation consequence
  - source_id: chunk-62a88f33f5bf6599aeaca364b95347b8<SEP>chunk-9ec36edffaf34ac9c5018481c45ac088<SEP>chunk-0692ecc0ed9739253244b6a78e8c9ece<SEP>chunk-d3bad11647e05d5d9b1c20ffb85b3797<SEP>chunk-5f510edda3c030eb7c2fe8108aa4220e<SEP>chunk-17d7927159a30e2bbf3dcbb81cfb3d2c<SEP>chunk-5f81ce728c86e723a0106ba652dfcbb5<SEP>chunk-a30a8f9076747b03c8bf9d65d8e1a0ca<SEP>chunk-f89d74a1d49c6cfcb55ae7d7cd3f79bc<SEP>chunk-204a6b534375fc21b3dd1bdbe1e52c5d<SEP>chunk-0b9abfa779f1ce4717b434fec4044da8<SEP>chunk-cfd30d4089d4f27dbf39824ba46f8303

Relation: Students -> Plagiarism
  - weight: 12.0
  - description: The relation "Plagiarism, Students" describes the consequences for students who commit plagiarism. The primary and consistent consequence across all descriptions is that the student will receive a grade of zero for the course in which the plagiarism occurred.  Beyond this academic penalty, the descriptions indicate that the incident typically triggers a formal institutional process. This process is variably described as the student facing a "formal review," "further review," or having the incident "submitted for review." Concurrently, most descriptions state that the plagiarism will be "reported" or that the student will "be reported," implying that a record of the misconduct is created and likely escalated to a relevant administrative body, such as an academic integrity office or a disciplinary committee. Therefore, the standard outcome for student plagiarism is a failing grade for the course accompanied by an official review and reporting of the violation.<SEP>Students committing plagiarism will receive a grade of zero for the course and be reported to school and university authorities.<SEP>Students who engage in plagiarism will receive a grade of zero for the course.<SEP>Students who commit plagiarism will be graded 0 for the course.<SEP>For any kind of plagiarism, students will be graded 0 for the course and the incident is submitted for review.
  - keywords: academic integrity,academic violation,disciplinary action,grading,grading consequence,penalty,violation,violation consequence
  - source_id: chunk-62a88f33f5bf6599aeaca364b95347b8<SEP>chunk-9ec36edffaf34ac9c5018481c45ac088<SEP>chunk-0692ecc0ed9739253244b6a78e8c9ece<SEP>chunk-d3bad11647e05d5d9b1c20ffb85b3797<SEP>chunk-5f510edda3c030eb7c2fe8108aa4220e<SEP>chunk-17d7927159a30e2bbf3dcbb81cfb3d2c<SEP>chunk-5f81ce728c86e723a0106ba652dfcbb5<SEP>chunk-a30a8f9076747b03c8bf9d65d8e1a0ca<SEP>chunk-f89d74a1d49c6cfcb55ae7d7cd3f79bc<SEP>chunk-204a6b534375fc21b3dd1bdbe1e52c5d<SEP>chunk-0b9abfa779f1ce4717b434fec4044da8<SEP>chunk-cfd30d4089d4f27dbf39824ba46f8303

Relation: Theory Sessions -> Exams
  - weight: 16.0
  - description: The relation between "Exams" and "Theory Sessions" is a formal academic policy that establishes attendance at theory sessions as a mandatory prerequisite for examination eligibility. The core, consistently stated rule is that a student who is absent from more than three theory sessions is barred, disqualified, or not permitted to take the course exams. This creates a direct conditional link where failure to meet the attendance requirement—specifically by exceeding the three-session threshold—results in the forfeiture of exam privileges. Descriptions uniformly characterize this policy as one where excessive absences automatically disqualify a student from taking the exams.<SEP>Students who are absent for more than three theory sessions are not allowed to take the exams.
  - keywords: academic assessment,academic consequence,academic eligibility,academic penalty,academic policy,attendance policy,attendance requirement,eligibility,eligibility requirement,prerequisite
  - source_id: chunk-fd7f30c34427bbb1c355045ce3e5082a<SEP>chunk-971be220bb86f5aa14877b4902bab258<SEP>chunk-a7f71d80abc6083184932aa30b343bb1<SEP>chunk-2bce7ac607b8b407722828c17e94282a<SEP>chunk-316f9be9c7846e523d2eea43d4923668<SEP>chunk-69b9176508fdf9d5ca323302a97875e5<SEP>chunk-62a88f33f5bf6599aeaca364b95347b8<SEP>chunk-8349910d2f3e3207a3f720b885c1be9e<SEP>chunk-a20a9abadf6e6db8ee68045a4fa77d97<SEP>chunk-dc817b239ade09690d81ceb8681bcac6<SEP>chunk-f75bcd02f33b99232c0789b5853ce3b3<SEP>chunk-f97280a6c8b34a5e9c7d8c2110c4c55c<SEP>chunk-f89d74a1d49c6cfcb55ae7d7cd3f79bc<SEP>chunk-204a6b534375fc21b3dd1bdbe1e52c5d<SEP>chunk-3f6d7c1d905ec03a165e715083fe5f6b<SEP>chunk-d8dbc93c6aebfa8acd8912513f6f4bd8
